speaker: Dhritarashtra
text_sanskrit: धृतराष्ट्र उवाच | धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे समवेता युयुत्सवः | मामकाः पाण्डवाश्चैव किमकुर्वत सञ्जय ||1||
transliteration: dhṛitarāśhtra uvācha | dharma-kṣhetre kuru-kṣhetre samavetā yuyutsavaḥ | māmakāḥ pāṇḍavāśhchaiva kimakurvata sañjaya ||
translation: Dhritarashtra said: O Sanjay, after gathering on the holy field of Kurukshetra, and desiring to fight, what did my sons and the sons of Pandu do?
commentary_full:
The two armies had gathered on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, well prepared to fight a war that was inevitable. Still, in this verse, King Dhritarashtra asked Sanjay what his sons and his brother Pandu’s sons were doing on the battlefield, even though it was apparent that they would fight.

The blind King Dhritarashtra’s attachment to his own sons had clouded his spiritual wisdom and deviated him from the path of virtue. He had usurped the kingdom of Hastinapur from the rightful heirs, the Pandavas, sons of his brother Pandu. Feeling guilty about the injustice he had done to his nephews, his conscience worried him about the outcome of the war.

The words *dharma-kṣhetre* (the land of dharma) reflect Dhritarashtra’s inner dilemma. Kurukshetra is described in the Shatapath Brahman as *kurukṣhetraṁ deva yajanam*, meaning the sacrificial arena of the celestial gods. Hence, it was regarded as sacred land that nourishes righteousness.

Dhritarashtra feared that this holy land might influence the minds of his sons. If their sense of discrimination awakened, they might refrain from killing their cousins and seek a peaceful settlement. Such a truce would mean the Pandavas would remain a threat to his sons. Though uncertain of the war’s outcome, Dhritarashtra still desired the battle to proceed and therefore asked Sanjay about the actions of both armies on the battlefield.
commentary_summary: Dhritarashtra’s question reflects his inner conflict, guilt, and fear. Though war was inevitable, his attachment to his sons and concern about the sacred nature of Kurukshetra made him anxious about the outcome.
theme: Dhritarashtra’s dilemma and anxiety before the war

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: सञ्जय उवाच । दृष्ट्वा तु पाण्डवानीकं व्यूढं दुर्योधनस्तदा । आचार्यमुपसङ्गम्य राजा वचनमब्रवीत् ।। 2।।
transliteration: sañjaya uvācha | dṛiṣhṭvā tu pāṇḍavānīkaṁ vyūḍhaṁ duryodhanastadā | āchāryamupasaṅgamya rājā vachanamabravīt ||
translation: Sanjay said: On observing the Pandava army standing in military formation, King Duryodhan approached his teacher Dronacharya and spoke the following words.
commentary_full:
Sanjay understood Dhritarashtra’s anxiety and sought to reassure him by explaining that the Pandava army was already arranged in a proper military formation, ready for battle. He then described the actions of Dhritarashtra’s son, Duryodhana, on the battlefield.

Although Dhritarashtra was the king, his blindness resulted in Duryodhana effectively ruling Hastinapur. Duryodhana is portrayed in the Mahabharata as arrogant, egoistic, cruel, and deeply envious of the Pandavas. From childhood, he harbored intense hatred toward them and believed that eliminating them was necessary to rule the kingdom unopposed.

However, upon witnessing the vast and well-organized Pandava army, Duryodhana was shaken. He had gravely underestimated their strength and was startled by the scale of their military preparation.

Duryodhana then approached his guru, Dronacharya, under the guise of showing respect. In reality, his intention was to pacify his own fear and insecurity. This act reveals that the sight of the Pandava forces deeply unsettled him and that he was now anxious about the outcome of the war.

The subsequent nine verses of the Bhagavad Gita are spoken by Duryodhana.
commentary_summary: Seeing the powerful and well-organized Pandava army, Duryodhana becomes uneasy. Approaching his teacher Dronacharya reflects his inner fear and insecurity about the impending war.
theme: Duryodhana’s fear upon seeing the Pandava army

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: पश्यैतां पाण्डुपुत्राणामाचार्य महतीं चमूम् । व्यूढां द्रुपदपुत्रेण तव शिष्येण धीमता ।। 3।।
transliteration: paśhyaitāṁ pāṇḍu-putrāṇām āchārya mahatīṁ chamūm | vyūḍhāṁ drupada-putreṇa tava śhiṣhyeṇa dhīmatā ||
translation: Duryodhan said: Respected teacher! Behold the mighty army of the sons of Pandu, so expertly arrayed for battle by your own gifted disciple, the son of Drupad.
commentary_full:
Duryodhana draws Dronacharya’s attention to the impressive and well-organized military formation of the Pandava army. He emphasizes that the army is commanded by Dhrishtadyumna, the son of King Drupad, who was also a disciple of Dronacharya.

Through this statement, Duryodhana subtly reminds his teacher of a past incident. In earlier times, Dronacharya, along with the Pandavas, had defeated King Drupad and seized half of his kingdom. To avenge this humiliation, Drupad performed a sacrificial ritual to obtain a son destined to kill Dronacharya. From this sacrifice, Dhrishtadyumna was born with a divine boon to slay him.

Despite knowing this fate, when Dhrishtadyumna approached Dronacharya for military training, the teacher accepted him with humility and imparted his knowledge without bias. Duryodhana now highlights this fact to provoke a sense of caution in Dronacharya.

By stressing that Dhrishtadyumna is both Drupad’s son and Dronacharya’s disciple, Duryodhana aims to ensure that his teacher does not show leniency or emotional attachment on the battlefield. His words reveal insecurity and an attempt to influence Dronacharya’s loyalty in the coming war.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana points out that the Pandava army is led by Dhrishtadyumna, Dronacharya’s own disciple. He subtly warns his teacher against showing compassion, revealing his fear and insecurity.
theme: Duryodhana’s insecurity and attempt to influence his teacher

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit:
अत्र शूरा महेष्वासा भीमार्जुनसमा युधि | युयुधानो विराटश्च द्रुपदश्च महारथ: || 4||
धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितान: काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान् | पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुङ्गव: || 5||
युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान् | सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथा: || 6||
transliteration:
atra śhūrā maheṣhvāsā bhīmārjuna-samā yudhi | yuyudhāno virāṭaśhcha drupadaśhcha mahā-rathaḥ ||
dhṛiṣhṭaketuśhchekitānaḥ kāśhirājaśhcha vīryavān | purujit kuntibhojaśhcha śhaibyaśhcha nara-puṅgavaḥ ||
yudhāmanyuśhcha vikrānta uttamaujāśhcha vīryavān | saubhadro draupadeyāśhcha sarva eva mahā-rathāḥ ||
translation: Duryodhan said: Behold in the Pandava ranks many powerful warriors, wielding mighty bows and equal in prowess to Bheem and Arjun—Yuyudhan, Virat, and Drupad. There are also heroic fighters like Dhrishtaketu, Chekitan, the gallant King of Kashi, Purujit, Kuntibhoj, and Shaibya—the best among men. Added to them are the courageous Yudhamanyu, the valiant Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi, all of whom are great warrior chiefs.
commentary_full:
Overcome by anxiety, Duryodhana perceives the Pandava army as far more formidable than he had anticipated. The sight of such a vast assembly of elite warriors shakes his confidence and heightens his fear of defeat.

In an attempt to express and perhaps justify his apprehension, Duryodhana begins listing the prominent mahārathīs on the Pandava side—warriors capable of single-handedly combating ten thousand ordinary soldiers. Each name he mentions represents exceptional valor, strength, and leadership.

By emphasizing that these warriors are equal in might to Bheem and Arjun, Duryodhana unconsciously admits the gravity of the threat before him. His enumeration reflects not pride but insecurity, revealing how deeply unsettled he is by the military power amassed against him.

These verses expose Duryodhana’s inner turmoil. While outwardly confident, inwardly he is gripped by fear and doubt, aware that the battle he has instigated may lead to catastrophic consequences.
commentary_summary: Fearing defeat, Duryodhana lists the mighty warriors on the Pandava side. His detailed enumeration reveals anxiety, insecurity, and realization of the formidable strength he faces.
theme: Duryodhana’s fear and recognition of the Pandavas’ formidable strength

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम | नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते || 7||
transliteration: asmākaṁ tu viśhiṣhṭā ye tānnibodha dwijottama | nāyakā mama sainyasya sanjñārthaṁ tānbravīmi te ||
translation: Duryodhan said: O best of Brahmins, hear too about the principal generals on our side, who are especially qualified to lead. These I now recount unto you.
commentary_full:
Although Dronacharya was primarily a teacher of military science and not a frontline warrior, he stood on the battlefield as one of the chief commanders of the Kaurava army. Duryodhana, insecure and distrustful by nature, even harbored doubts about the loyalty of his own preceptor.

By deliberately addressing Dronacharya as *dwijottama* (best among the twice-born Brahmins), Duryodhana employed a subtle and veiled insult. His words carried an implicit taunt—if Dronacharya failed to demonstrate valor in battle, he would be regarded merely as a Brahmin attached to comfort, fine food, and royal patronage, rather than a true warrior-teacher.

Sensing the harshness of his own statement and to bolster both his teacher’s morale and his own confidence, Duryodhana quickly shifted the focus. He began enumerating the distinguished generals on the Kaurava side, highlighting their courage and military prowess.

This verse thus reveals Duryodhana’s manipulative nature, his insecurity about loyalty, and his attempt to psychologically pressure Dronacharya while outwardly showing respect.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana subtly questions Dronacharya’s loyalty using veiled words, then shifts to naming the Kaurava generals to boost confidence, revealing manipulation and insecurity.
theme: Duryodhana’s manipulation and doubt toward his own commander

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जय: | अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च || 8||
transliteration: bhavānbhīṣhmaśhcha karṇaśhcha kṛipaśhcha samitiñjayaḥ | aśhvatthāmā vikarṇaśhcha saumadattis tathaiva cha ||
translation: Duryodhan said: There are personalities like yourself, Bheeshma, Karna, Kripa, Ashwatthama, Vikarna, and Bhurishrava, who are ever victorious in battle.
commentary_full:
After subtly pressuring Dronacharya, Duryodhana begins listing the great warriors on the Kaurava side to instill confidence and assert their superiority. He first mentions Dronacharya himself, followed by legendary fighters such as Bhishma and Karna, whose reputations as invincible warriors were widely acknowledged.

By referring to these warriors as *samitiñjayaḥ*—those who conquer in battle—Duryodhana attempts to counterbalance the fear he felt upon seeing the Pandava army. Each name he lists represents immense strength, experience, and loyalty to the Kaurava cause.

This enumeration serves a dual purpose. Outwardly, it is meant to encourage Dronacharya and reinforce unity among the commanders. Inwardly, it reveals Duryodhana’s need for reassurance, as he depends heavily on these mighty warriors to secure victory.

Thus, while the verse appears confident, it subtly reflects Duryodhana’s psychological dependence on the prowess of his generals to overcome his inner fear.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana lists the mighty warriors on the Kaurava side to boost confidence and reassure himself, revealing dependence on their strength despite his inner fear.
theme: Duryodhana’s reliance on powerful warriors for reassurance

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: अन्ये च बहव: शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविता: | नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणा: सर्वे युद्धविशारदा: || 9||
transliteration: anye cha bahavaḥ śhūrā madarthe tyaktajīvitāḥ | nānā-śhastra-praharaṇāḥ sarve yuddha-viśhāradāḥ ||
translation: Duryodhan said: Also, there are many other heroic warriors who are prepared to lay down their lives for my sake. They are all skilled in the art of warfare and equipped with various kinds of weapons.
commentary_full:
Continuing his attempt to strengthen morale, Duryodhana speaks of the many unnamed warriors on the Kaurava side who are willing to sacrifice their lives for his cause. By emphasizing their readiness to die for him, he seeks to project absolute loyalty and devotion toward himself as their leader.

These warriors are described as being proficient in warfare and well-armed with diverse weapons, suggesting that the Kaurava army is not only strong because of its famous commanders, but also because of the collective strength and discipline of its soldiers.

However, the phrase *madarthe tyaktajīvitāḥ* (ready to give up their lives for my sake) subtly exposes Duryodhana’s ego and insecurity. He frames the war as being fought for himself rather than for dharma or justice, revealing his self-centered mindset.

Thus, while the verse outwardly conveys confidence and strength, it inwardly reflects Duryodhana’s need for reassurance and his reliance on the blind loyalty of his followers to sustain his resolve.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana highlights the loyalty and readiness of many warriors to die for him, revealing both confidence in numbers and an underlying ego-driven insecurity.
theme: Duryodhana’s reliance on loyal warriors and ego-driven confidence

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: अपर्याप्तं तदस्माकं बलं भीष्माभिरक्षितम् | पर्याप्तं त्विदमेतेषां बलं भीमाभिरक्षितम् || 10||
transliteration: aparyāptaṁ tadasmākaṁ balaṁ bhīṣhmābhirakṣhitam | paryāptaṁ tvidameteṣhāṁ balaṁ bhīmābhirakṣhitam ||
translation: Duryodhan said: The strength of our army is unlimited and safely marshalled by Grandsire Bheeshma, whereas the strength of the Pandava army, carefully marshalled by Bheem, is limited.
commentary_full:
Duryodhana compares the military strength of the two armies, expressing great confidence in the Kaurava forces under the command of Grandsire Bheeshma. Bheeshma, the commander-in-chief, was not only a legendary warrior but also possessed the boon of choosing the time of his death, which made him virtually invincible in battle. This gave Duryodhana a strong sense of security and pride.

In contrast, Duryodhana downplays the Pandava army by stating that it is limited in strength, even though it was being marshalled by Bheem, his formidable and sworn enemy. This comparison reflects Duryodhana’s ego and overconfidence rather than an objective assessment of reality.

The proverb *vinaash kāle viparīta buddhi*—when destruction approaches, the intellect becomes perverted—aptly applies here. Instead of humility and caution, Duryodhana indulges in self-glorification, blind to the deeper forces at play.

Although Bheeshma led the Kaurava army, his loyalty was complex. As the eldest member of the Kuru dynasty, he cared deeply for both the Kauravas and the Pandavas. Bound by his vow to serve the throne of Hastinapur, he fought on the Kaurava side despite being aware of their injustice.

Bheeshma also knew that Lord Krishna Himself was present on the Pandava side, signifying that dharma was with them. He understood that no force could ultimately make adharma victorious. Yet, to honor his lifelong vows, Bheeshma led the Kaurava army, highlighting his moral strength, inner conflict, and enigmatic character.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana boasts about the Kaurava army’s strength under Bheeshma, revealing overconfidence and ego, while ignoring the deeper truth that dharma—and Krishna—stand with the Pandavas.
theme: Duryodhana’s overconfidence contrasted with the deeper reality of dharma

speaker: Duryodhana
text_sanskrit: अयनेषु च सर्वेषु यथाभागमवस्थिता: | भीष्ममेवाभिरक्षन्तु भवन्त: सर्व एव हि || 11||
transliteration: ayaneṣhu cha sarveṣhu yathā-bhāgamavasthitāḥ | bhīṣhmamevābhirakṣhantu bhavantaḥ sarva eva hi ||
translation: Duryodhan said: Therefore, I call upon all the generals of the Kaurava army to remain at their respective strategic positions and give full protection and support to Grandsire Bheeshma.
commentary_full:
Having expressed his confidence in the strength of the Kaurava army under Bheeshma’s command, Duryodhana now issues a direct instruction to his generals. He urges them to stay firmly positioned at their assigned strategic points in the battle formation while ensuring complete protection of Grandsire Bheeshma.

Duryodhana regarded Bheeshma as the backbone of the Kaurava forces. Due to Bheeshma’s invincibility and unmatched experience, Duryodhana believed that as long as he was well supported, victory would be assured. Hence, he wanted the entire army to draw strength and inspiration from Bheeshma’s presence.

This command, however, also reveals Duryodhana’s inner fear. His insistence on protecting Bheeshma indicates his awareness that the morale and stability of the Kaurava army depended heavily on him. If Bheeshma were weakened or distracted, the confidence of the entire force could collapse.

Thus, this verse exposes Duryodhana’s strategic dependence on Bheeshma, combining outward authority with inward insecurity.
commentary_summary: Duryodhana instructs the Kaurava generals to protect Bheeshma at all costs, revealing both strategic planning and deep reliance on Bheeshma for morale and strength.
theme: Duryodhana’s strategic dependence on Bheeshma

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तस्य सञ्जनयन्हर्षं कुरुवृद्ध: पितामह: | सिंहनादं विनद्योच्चै: शङ्खं दध्मौ प्रतापवान् || 12||
transliteration: tasya sañjanayan harṣhaṁ kuru-vṛiddhaḥ pitāmahaḥ | siṁha-nādaṁ vinadyochchaiḥ śhaṅkhaṁ dadhmau pratāpavān ||
translation: Sanjay said: Then, the grand old man of the Kuru dynasty, the glorious patriarch Bheeshma, roared like a lion and blew his conch shell very loudly, giving joy to Duryodhan.
commentary_full:
Bheeshma clearly understood that Duryodhana’s confidence was fragile and that the presence of the Supreme Lord Krishna on the Pandava side meant ultimate victory for dharma. Yet, bound by his vows and his responsibility toward the throne of Hastinapur, Bheeshma chose to perform his duty as the commander of the Kaurava army.

Sensing Duryodhana’s anxiety, Bheeshma blew his conch shell with a sound likened to a lion’s roar. In ancient warfare, the blowing of the conch marked the formal commencement of battle and also served as a powerful psychological signal to inspire one’s own troops.

Through this act, Bheeshma reassured Duryodhana that he would stand firmly by the Kaurava side and lead the army with full commitment, despite his inner awareness of the moral imbalance of the war. His gesture temporarily uplifted Duryodhana’s spirits and strengthened the morale of the Kaurava forces.

This verse highlights Bheeshma’s noble character—his compassion for his grand-nephew, unwavering sense of duty, and willingness to act selflessly even in a conflict where his heart was divided.
commentary_summary: To encourage the anxious Duryodhana, Bheeshma blows his conch shell loudly, signaling the start of war and reaffirming his dutiful leadership despite his inner conflict.
theme: Bheeshma’s duty-bound encouragement amid moral conflict

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तत: शङ्खाश्च भेर्यश्च पणवानकगोमुखा: | सहसैवाभ्यहन्यन्त स शब्दस्तुमुलोऽभवत् || 13||
transliteration: tataḥ śhaṅkhāśhcha bheryaśhcha paṇavānaka-gomukhāḥ | sahasaivābhyahanyanta sa śhabdastumulo ’bhavat ||
translation: Sanjay said: Thereafter, conches, kettledrums, bugles, trumpets, and horns suddenly blared forth, and their combined sound was overwhelming.
commentary_full:
Responding to Bheeshma’s powerful signal to commence battle, the entire Kaurava army erupted in sound. Soldiers and commanders alike began blowing conches and playing drums, kettledrums, bugles, trumpets, and horns simultaneously.

Each instrument had a distinct role in ancient warfare—conches to signal readiness, drums and kettledrums to set rhythm and courage, bugles and horns to convey commands across the battlefield. When sounded together, they produced a deafening and tumultuous roar.

This overwhelming sound symbolized the formal beginning of the war and was meant to instill enthusiasm and confidence among the Kaurava forces, while also intimidating the opposing army. The verse captures the charged atmosphere and collective momentum of the battlefield at the moment war was declared.
commentary_summary: Following Bheeshma’s signal, the Kaurava army blows conches and instruments together, creating a thunderous sound that marks the start of the war.
theme: The dramatic commencement of the great war

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तत: श्वेतैर्हयैर्युक्ते महति स्यन्दने स्थितौ | माधव: पाण्डवश्चैव दिव्यौ शङ्खौ प्रदध्मतु: || 14||
transliteration: tataḥ śhvetairhayairyukte mahati syandane sthitau | mādhavaḥ pāṇḍavaśhchaiva divyau śhaṅkhau pradadhmatuḥ ||
translation: Sanjay said: Then, from amidst the Pandava army, seated in a glorious chariot drawn by white horses, Madhav and Arjun blew their divine conch shells.
commentary_full:
As the tumultuous sound from the Kaurava side began to subside, a powerful response arose from the Pandava army. Seated on a magnificent chariot drawn by white horses, Lord Shree Krishna and Arjun blew their divine conch shells with confidence and fearlessness, igniting fresh enthusiasm among the Pandava forces.

Sanjay addresses Lord Krishna here as *Madhav*. The word *Mā* refers to Goddess Lakshmi, the goddess of prosperity, and *dhav* means husband. Goddess Lakshmi is the consort of Lord Vishnu, of whom Krishna is an incarnation. By using this name, the verse subtly conveys that prosperity, fortune, and divine grace stood with the Pandavas.

The term *Pandava* refers to the sons of King Pandu. In this verse, it specifically denotes Arjun, the third of the five brothers. Arjun was a supremely skilled archer and a mighty warrior. His splendid chariot, gifted by Agni, the celestial god of fire, further symbolizes divine support.

This moment marks a spiritual and moral counterpoint to the Kaurava uproar. Unlike the noise driven by ego and fear, the Pandava sound resonates with confidence rooted in dharma and divine presence.
commentary_summary: Krishna and Arjun blow their divine conches from a glorious chariot, signaling divine support, prosperity, and renewed confidence for the Pandava army.
theme: Divine presence and moral strength of the Pandavas

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: पाञ्चजन्यं हृषीकेशो देवदत्तं धनञ्जय: | पौण्ड्रं दध्मौ महाशङ्खं भीमकर्मा वृकोदर: || 15||
transliteration: pāñchajanyaṁ hṛiṣhīkeśho devadattaṁ dhanañjayaḥ | pauṇḍraṁ dadhmau mahā-śhaṅkhaṁ bhīma-karmā vṛikodaraḥ ||
translation: Sanjay said: Hrishikesh blew his conch shell called Panchajanya, Arjun blew his conch Devadatta, and Bheem, the performer of herculean deeds, blew his mighty conch named Paundra.
commentary_full:
In this verse, Sanjay describes the powerful response of the leading Pandava warriors by naming their individual conch shells. Lord Shree Krishna is addressed as *Hrishikesh*, meaning the Lord of the mind and senses. This title signifies Krishna’s complete mastery over the inner faculties of all beings, highlighting His divine nature.

Krishna blew his conch Panchajanya, symbolizing divine authority and control. Arjun, referred to as *Dhananjaya*—the conqueror of wealth—blew his conch Devadatta, representing strength earned through discipline and righteousness.

Bheem, described as *Vrikodara* (the voracious eater) and *Bhīma-karmā* (one capable of herculean deeds), blew his mighty conch Paundra. This reflects his immense physical power and fearless nature.

The naming of each conch and its bearer emphasizes the individuality, valor, and divine support of the Pandava side. Unlike the collective uproar of the Kauravas, the Pandava conches resound with clarity, purpose, and spiritual authority.
commentary_summary: Krishna, Arjun, and Bheem each blow their distinct conch shells, symbolizing divine control, disciplined strength, and immense power on the Pandava side.
theme: Divine leadership and individual valor of the Pandavas

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit:
अनन्तविजयं राजा कुन्तीपुत्रो युधिष्ठिर: | नकुल: सहदेवश्च सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ || 16||
काश्यश्च परमेष्वास: शिखण्डी च महारथ: | धृष्टद्युम्नो विराटश्च सात्यकिश्चापराजित: || 17||
द्रुपदो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्वश: पृथिवीपते | सौभद्रश्च महाबाहु: शङ्खान्दध्मु: पृथक् पृथक् || 18||
transliteration:
anantavijayaṁ rājā kuntī-putro yudhiṣhṭhiraḥ | nakulaḥ sahadevaśhcha sughoṣha-maṇipuṣhpakau ||
kāśhyaśhcha parameṣhvāsaḥ śhikhaṇḍī cha mahā-rathaḥ | dhṛiṣhṭadyumno virāṭaśhcha sātyakiśh chāparājitaḥ ||
drupado draupadeyāśhcha sarvaśhaḥ pṛithivī-pate | saubhadraśhcha mahā-bāhuḥ śhaṅkhāndadhmuḥ pṛithak pṛithak ||
translation: Sanjay said: King Yudhishthir blew the conch Anantavijay; Nakul and Sahadev blew Sughosh and Manipushpak. The excellent archer, the king of Kashi, the great warrior Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virat, the invincible Satyaki, King Drupad, the sons of Draupadi, and the mighty-armed Abhimanyu, son of Subhadra, all blew their respective conch shells, O ruler of the earth.
commentary_full:
In these verses, Sanjay describes the powerful and organized response of the Pandava side as each prominent warrior blows his own conch shell. Yudhishthira, the eldest of the Pandavas, is respectfully addressed as ‘King,’ reflecting his innate nobility and righteousness. Whether living in royal comfort or enduring exile, Yudhishthira consistently embodied the qualities of a true ruler. His performance of the Rājasūya Yajña further established his status, earning him homage from other kings.

Nakul and Sahadev, the younger Pandava brothers, also participate confidently, symbolizing unity and collective strength. One by one, great warriors and kings allied with the Pandavas—such as the king of Kashi, Shikhandi, Dhrishtadyumna, Virat, Satyaki, Drupad, the sons of Draupadi, and Abhimanyu—blow their individual conches, signifying readiness, valor, and determination.

Sanjay addresses Dhritarashtra here as ‘Ruler of the earth,’ subtly reminding him of his responsibility. With rulers and princes from across the land aligned on both sides, the entire world seemed divided by this conflict. The war threatened immense and irreversible destruction. At this critical moment, Dhritarashtra alone possessed the authority to stop it. Through this narration, Sanjay implicitly questions whether the blind king would rise to his duty and prevent the catastrophe.
commentary_summary: The Pandava warriors blow their individual conch shells, displaying unity, righteousness, and strength. Sanjay subtly reminds Dhritarashtra of his duty as ruler to prevent the impending destruction.
theme: Pandava unity and the ruler’s responsibility to prevent destruction

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: स घोषो धार्तराष्ट्राणां हृदयानि व्यदारयत् | नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव तुमुलोऽभ्यनुनादयन् || 19||
transliteration: sa ghoṣho dhārtarāṣhṭrāṇāṁ hṛidayāni vyadārayat | nabhaśhcha pṛithivīṁ chaiva tumulo abhyanunādayan ||
translation: Sanjay said: The terrific sound thundered across the sky and the earth and shattered the hearts of your sons, O Dhritarashtra.
commentary_full:
Sanjay vividly describes the psychological impact of the Pandava army’s conch shells on the Kauravas. The thunderous sound echoed through the sky and the earth, symbolizing not just physical noise but the moral and spiritual force behind the Pandavas.

Significantly, Sanjay points out that this overwhelming sound shattered the hearts of Dhritarashtra’s sons alone. He does not mention any such effect on the Pandavas when the Kauravas made their uproar earlier. This contrast reveals the inner condition of the two sides.

The Kauravas were plagued by fear and inner turmoil because their conscience was burdened by injustice, greed, and wrongdoing. Though they relied heavily on physical strength and numbers, they lacked moral conviction. The sound of the Pandava conches awakened their latent fear and uncertainty.

The Pandavas, on the other hand, were calm and confident. With the Supreme Lord Shree Krishna on their side, they felt divinely protected. Their confidence was rooted in dharma, making them fearless in the face of war. This verse thus highlights that inner righteousness, not mere strength, determines true courage.
commentary_summary: The Pandava conches produce a thunderous sound that shakes the hearts of the Kauravas, revealing their inner fear, while the Pandavas remain confident due to their righteousness and divine support.
theme: Inner fear of adharma versus confidence born of dharma

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: अथ व्यवस्थितान्दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान् कपिध्वज: | प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डव: ||20|| हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते |
transliteration: atha vyavasthitān dṛiṣhṭvā dhārtarāṣhṭrān kapi-dhwajaḥ | pravṛitte śhastra-sampāte dhanurudyamya pāṇḍavaḥ || hṛiṣhīkeśhaṁ tadā vākyam idam āha mahī-pate |
translation: Sanjay said: At that time, the son of Pandu, Arjun, bearing the emblem of Hanuman on his chariot flag, lifted his bow. Seeing your sons arrayed for battle, O King, Arjun then spoke the following words to Shree Krishna.
commentary_full:
In this verse, Sanjay introduces a significant turning point—Arjun’s first words before the battle truly begins. Arjun is addressed as *Kapi-dhwaja*, meaning ‘one whose chariot bears the monkey flag.’ This signifies the presence and protection of the mighty Hanuman on Arjun’s chariot.

The origin of this symbol traces back to an incident where Arjun once became proud of his archery skills and boasted before Shree Krishna that he could have built a bridge of arrows during Lord Rama’s time, instead of the monkeys laboring with stones. Shree Krishna, knowing the lesson Arjun needed to learn, asked him to demonstrate such a bridge.

Arjun skillfully constructed a massive bridge of arrows. However, when Hanuman was invited to test it, the bridge began to collapse under his weight. Realizing his arrogance and limitation, Arjun humbly sought forgiveness. Hanuman then taught him the value of humility and granted him a boon—that he would remain seated on Arjun’s chariot during the great war to protect him.

Thus, as Arjun lifts his bow and looks upon the Kaurava army, he stands not merely as a warrior, but as one who has learned humility through experience. This moment sets the stage for Arjun’s inner conflict and his forthcoming dialogue with Shree Krishna, which becomes the foundation of the Bhagavad Gita.
commentary_summary: As the battle is about to begin, Arjun lifts his bow and turns to Krishna. His monkey-bannered chariot symbolizes Hanuman’s protection and reminds Arjun of the humility he learned before the war.
theme: Arjun’s readiness for battle and the lesson of humility before action

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: अर्जुन उवाच | सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये रथं स्थापय मेऽच्युत || 21|| यावदेतान्निरीक्षेऽहं योद्धुकामानवस्थितान् | कैर्मया सह योद्धव्यमस्मिन् रणसमुद्यमे || 22||
transliteration: arjuna uvācha | senayor ubhayor madhye rathaṁ sthāpaya me ’chyuta || yāvadetān nirīkṣhe ’haṁ yoddhu-kāmān avasthitān | kairmayā saha yoddhavyam asmin raṇa-samudyame ||
translation: Arjun said: O Infallible One, please take my chariot to the middle of both armies, so that I may look at the warriors arrayed for battle, with whom I must fight in this great combat.
commentary_full:
Arjun, a supremely skilled warrior protected by Hanuman seated on his chariot flag, now takes an unexpected role—he instructs Shree Krishna, the Supreme Lord Himself, who has humbly agreed to become his charioteer. Addressing Him as *Achyuta*, the infallible and ever-dependable One, Arjun requests Krishna to place the chariot between the two armies.

This moment reveals the depth of devotion between Arjun and Krishna. Though Krishna is the Supreme, He willingly submits to His devotee’s request. Arjun wishes to closely observe those assembled for battle, not out of arrogance, but to understand whom he is about to fight in this grave war.

The relationship shown here reflects a profound spiritual truth: the Lord becomes bound by the love of His devotees. As expressed in the Bhagavatam (9.4.63), although God is supremely independent, He allows Himself to be controlled by pure devotion. This verse marks the beginning of Arjun’s introspection, which soon leads to his inner conflict and the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita.
commentary_summary: Arjun asks Krishna to place the chariot between the armies so he may see his opponents. This reveals their deep devotee–Lord relationship and sets the stage for Arjun’s inner conflict.
theme: Devotee–Lord relationship and the beginning of Arjun’s inner conflict

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: योत्स्यमानानवेक्षेऽहं य एतेऽत्र समागता: | धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेर्युद्धे प्रियचिकीर्षव: || 23||
transliteration: yotsyamānān avekṣhe ’haṁ ya ete ’tra samāgatāḥ | dhārtarāṣhṭrasya durbuddher yuddhe priya-chikīrṣhavaḥ ||
translation: Arjun said: I desire to see those who have assembled here to fight, wishing to please the evil-minded son of Dhritarashtra.
commentary_full:
At this moment, Arjun’s mindset is still firm and resolute. With the Supreme Lord Shree Krishna as his charioteer and Hanuman protecting his chariot, he feels fearless and confident. He clearly recognizes the moral divide of the battlefield—one side stands for justice, while the other has aligned itself with injustice.

Arjun views the Pandavas as the rightful heirs to half the kingdom of Hastinapur, a claim denied by the Kauravas out of greed and malice. His intention in asking Krishna to place the chariot between the armies is to carefully observe those who have consciously chosen to support the unrighteous cause of Dhritarashtra’s sons.

At this stage, Arjun’s outlook is that anyone who stands with adharma deserves to face the consequences. He believes that by siding with the wicked, these warriors have forfeited mercy. This verse reflects Arjun’s warrior spirit and sense of justice before compassion and emotional conflict begin to arise in the subsequent verses.
commentary_summary: Arjun wishes to see those who have chosen to support the unjust Kaurava cause. At this stage, he is confident, resolute, and ready to punish those aligned with adharma.
theme: Arjun’s firm resolve against injustice before inner conflict arises

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: सञ्जय उवाच | एवमुक्तो हृषीकेशो गुडाकेशेन भारत | सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये स्थापयित्वा रथोत्तमम् || 24||
transliteration: sañjaya uvācha | evam ukto hṛiṣhīkeśho guḍākeśhena bhārata | senayor ubhayor madhye sthāpayitvā rathottamam ||
translation: Sanjay said: O Dhritarashtra, having thus been addressed by Arjun, the conqueror of sleep, Shree Krishna placed the magnificent chariot between the two armies.
commentary_full:
Sanjay narrates how Shree Krishna immediately complied with Arjun’s request. Addressed by Arjun, who is here called *Guḍākeśa*—the conqueror of sleep—Krishna drew the splendid chariot to the center of the battlefield, between the two opposing armies.

This act once again highlights the extraordinary bond between the Supreme Lord and His devotee. Though Krishna is the Lord of the senses (*Hṛiṣhīkeśa*), He willingly accepts the role of a charioteer and follows Arjun’s instruction without hesitation.

In this verse, Sanjay addresses Dhritarashtra as *Bhārata*, meaning a descendant of the great King Bharat. This form of address subtly reminds Dhritarashtra of his noble lineage and the responsibility that comes with it. As a ruler born in such an illustrious dynasty, he was expected to uphold dharma and prevent injustice.

Krishna positioning the chariot between the two armies sets the stage for Arjun’s emotional turmoil and the profound spiritual dialogue that follows, which ultimately becomes the Bhagavad Gita.
commentary_summary: Krishna obeys Arjun’s request and places the chariot between the armies, while Sanjay subtly reminds Dhritarashtra of his noble lineage and responsibility.
theme: Divine humility and the unfolding of Arjun’s inner conflict

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखत: सर्वेषां च महीक्षिताम् | उवाच पार्थ पश्यैतान्समवेतान्कुरूनिति || 25||
transliteration: bhīṣhma-droṇa-pramukhataḥ sarveṣhāṁ cha mahī-kṣhitām | uvācha pārtha paśhyaitān samavetān kurūn iti ||
translation: Sanjay said: In the presence of Bheeshma, Dronacharya, and all the other kings, Shree Krishna said: O Parth, behold these Kurus gathered here.
commentary_full:
Shree Krishna addresses Arjun as *Partha*, the son of Pritha (Kunti), gently invoking his maternal identity. Standing before mighty elders like Bheeshma and Dronacharya, and surrounded by many kings, Krishna deliberately uses the word *Kurus* to describe those assembled on the opposing side.

By doing so, Krishna reminds Arjun that the warriors he is eager to fight are not merely enemies, but his own kin—descendants of the same ancestral lineage of King Kuru. Both the Pandavas and the Kauravas belong to this single family.

This moment is pivotal. Krishna subtly begins to awaken Arjun’s emotional awareness, planting the seed of inner conflict. The Lord, being omniscient, intentionally allows this confusion to arise so that it may later be resolved through higher wisdom.

Thus, this verse marks the quiet beginning of Arjun’s moral dilemma. Krishna is preparing the ground for the profound spiritual teaching of the Bhagavad Gita, which will transcend the battlefield and guide future generations.
commentary_summary: Krishna points out that the warriors facing Arjun are his own relatives, gently initiating the inner conflict that will lead to the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita.
theme: The awakening of Arjun’s inner conflict through recognition of kinship

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान् पार्थ: पितृ नथ पितामहान् | आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातृ न्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा || 26|| श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि |
transliteration: tatrāpaśhyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝīn atha pitāmahān | āchāryān mātulān bhrātṝīn putrān pautrān sakhīṁs tathā || śhvaśhurān suhṛidaśh chaiva senayor ubhayor api |
translation: Sanjay said: There, Arjun saw stationed in both armies his fathers, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, friends, fathers-in-law, and well-wishers.
commentary_full:
Standing between the two armies, Arjun’s perception undergoes a profound transformation. What moments earlier appeared as opposing forces now reveals itself as a gathering of his own people—family members, revered elders, teachers, and dear companions spread across both sides of the battlefield.

The presence of fathers and grandfathers evokes respect and gratitude, teachers awaken reverence, brothers and friends stir affection, while sons and future generations bring a deep sense of responsibility. Arjun realizes that the war will not merely decide political power but will irreversibly destroy familial bonds and the moral fabric of society.

This overwhelming sight dissolves Arjun’s earlier certainty. His warrior identity clashes with his human emotions, and compassion begins to overpower duty. The battlefield transforms into a space of moral questioning rather than mere combat.

This verse is the emotional turning point of the first chapter. It reveals the universal human dilemma—how does one act when duty conflicts with love? This inner collapse of Arjun becomes the gateway through which the timeless wisdom of the Bhagavad Gita will emerge.
commentary_summary: Seeing loved ones and respected elders on both sides of the war shatters Arjun’s resolve, awakening compassion and deep moral conflict.
theme: The clash between familial attachment and warrior duty leading to Arjun’s inner crisis

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तत्रापश्यत्स्थितान् पार्थ: पितृ नथ पितामहान् | आचार्यान्मातुलान्भ्रातृ न्पुत्रान्पौत्रान्सखींस्तथा || 26|| श्वशुरान्सुहृदश्चैव सेनयोरुभयोरपि |
transliteration: tatrāpaśhyat sthitān pārthaḥ pitṝīn atha pitāmahān | āchāryān mātulān bhrātṝīn putrān pautrān sakhīṁs tathā || śhvaśhurān suhṛidaśh chaiva senayor ubhayor api |
translation: Sanjay said: There, Arjun saw stationed in both armies his fathers, grandfathers, teachers, maternal uncles, brothers, sons, grandsons, friends, fathers-in-law, and well-wishers.
commentary_full:

The presence of fathers and grandfathers evokes respect and gratitude, teachers awaken reverence, brothers and friends stir affection, while sons and future generations bring a deep sense of responsibility. Arjun realizes that the war will not merely decide political power but will irreversibly destroy familial bonds and the moral fabric of society.

This overwhelming sight dissolves Arjun’s earlier certainty. His warrior identity clashes with his human emotions, and compassion begins to overpower duty. The battlefield transforms into a space of moral questioning rather than mere combat.

This verse is the emotional turning point of the first chapter. It reveals the universal human dilemma—how does one act when duty conflicts with love? This inner collapse of Arjun becomes the gateway through which the timeless wisdom of the Bhagavad Gita will emerge.

theme: The clash between familial attachment and warrior duty leading to Arjun’s inner crisis

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: तान्समीक्ष्य स कौन्तेय: सर्वान्बन्धूनवस्थितान् || 27|| कृपया परयाविष्टो विषीदन्निदमब्रवीत् |
transliteration: tān samīkṣhya sa kaunteyaḥ sarvān bandhūn avasthitān | kṛipayā parayāviṣhṭo viṣhīdann idam abravīt ||
translation: Sanjay said: Seeing all his relatives present there, Arjun, the son of Kunti, was overwhelmed with compassion and, filled with deep sorrow, spoke the following words.
commentary_full:
Shree Krishna’s deliberate words now bear their full effect. As Arjun surveys the battlefield, he no longer perceives enemies but recognizes them all as Kurus—his own kin bound by blood, memory, and shared history. The realization strikes his heart with immense force.

The fearless warrior who moments earlier was eager to punish injustice is suddenly overtaken by compassion. Visualizing the irreversible destruction this war would unleash—families shattered, elders slain, and generations erased—his courage begins to drain away.

Sanjay refers to him as *Kaunteya*, the son of Kunti, emphasizing Arjun’s softening heart. Like his mother, known for her compassion and sensitivity, Arjun’s emotional nature now surfaces strongly.

This verse marks the collapse of Arjun’s warrior certainty. Compassion eclipses valor, clarity gives way to confusion, and sorrow overwhelms resolve. His mind fills with doubt and questions, preparing the ground for his moral paralysis and the profound philosophical inquiry that follows.
commentary_summary: Seeing his relatives on both sides, Arjun is overcome by compassion and sorrow. His warrior resolve collapses, giving rise to confusion and deep emotional turmoil.
theme: Arjun’s emotional collapse as compassion overwhelms warrior duty

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: अर्जुन उवाच | दृष्ट्वेमं स्वजनं कृष्ण युयुत्सुं समुपस्थितम् || 28|| सीदन्ति मम गात्राणि मुखं च परिशुष्यति |
transliteration: arjuna uvācha | dṛiṣhṭvemaṁ sva-janaṁ kṛiṣhṇa yuyutsuṁ samupasthitam || sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṁ cha pariśhuṣhyati |
translation: Arjun said: O Krishna, seeing my own kinsmen assembled here and eager for battle, my limbs are weakening and my mouth is drying up.
commentary_full:
Arjun now openly expresses the intense physical and psychological impact of his inner turmoil. Seeing his own relatives—family members, friends, and loved ones—standing before him with weapons, ready to fight and die, overwhelms his mind and body.

His emotional shock manifests physically: his limbs tremble and lose strength, and his mouth dries up. These are classic symptoms of extreme anxiety, fear, and grief. The mighty warrior who once stood fearless is now shaken at the very core of his being.

The root cause of Arjun’s suffering lies in bodily attachment. Identifying himself solely with the physical body, he sees the destruction of his relatives as unbearable loss. In doing so, he momentarily forgets his spiritual identity—that the soul is eternal and distinct from the body.

Such material attachment inevitably brings sorrow, fear, pain, and despair. Bound by ignorance, the mind clings to temporary relationships and forgets the eternal truth that the Supreme Lord alone is the permanent well-wisher, friend, protector, and beloved of the soul.

This verse marks a critical descent into Arjun’s existential crisis, where emotional attachment eclipses spiritual wisdom, preparing the stage for Krishna’s transformative teachings.
commentary_summary: Overwhelmed by attachment to his relatives, Arjun experiences physical weakness and anxiety. His identification with the body causes fear and sorrow, signaling deep spiritual confusion.
theme: Arjun’s physical and emotional breakdown due to attachment and loss of spiritual awareness

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: वेपथुश्च शरीरे मे रोमहर्षश्च जायते || 29|| गाण्डीवं स्रंसते हस्तात्त्वक्चै व परिदह्यते | न च शक्नोम्यवस्थातुं भ्रमतीव च मे मन: || 30|| निमित्तानि च पश्यामि विपरीतानि केशव | न च श्रेयोऽनुपश्यामि हत्वा स्वजनमाहवे || 31||
transliteration: vepathuśh cha śharīre me roma-harṣhaśh cha jāyate || gāṇḍīvaṁ sraṁsate hastāt tvak chaiva paridahyate | na cha śhaknomy avasthātuṁ bhramatīva cha me manaḥ || nimittāni cha paśhyāmi viparītāni keśhava | na cha śhreyo ’nupaśhyāmi hatvā sva-janam āhave ||
translation: Arjun said: My body trembles and my hair stands on end. My bow, the Gāṇḍīv, slips from my hand and my skin burns. I am unable to steady myself; my mind reels in confusion. O Krishna, slayer of the Keshi demon, I see only inauspicious signs. I do not foresee any good in killing my own kinsmen in this battle.
commentary_full:
Arjun’s inner conflict now manifests as an intense psychosomatic breakdown. The warrior’s anguish spills into his body—trembling limbs, hair standing on end, burning skin, and the slipping of his famed bow Gāṇḍīv from his grasp. These physical signs mirror a mind overwhelmed by fear, sorrow, and moral distress.

Addressing Krishna as *Keśhava*, Arjun contrasts the Lord’s ability to slay demons with his own inability to harm those bound to him by love and blood. The very thought of killing relatives paralyzes him, eroding his confidence and clarity.

His mind whirls, unable to anchor itself in resolve. In this vulnerable state, Arjun begins interpreting the situation through ominous signs and portents, indicating a slide into anxiety-driven superstition. He foresees only misfortune and sin arising from the war, concluding that no righteous outcome can result from fratricide.

This passage marks the nadir of Arjun’s resolve. The heroic archer stands disarmed not by enemies, but by attachment and moral dread—creating the urgent need for spiritual guidance that Krishna will soon provide.
commentary_summary: Arjun experiences severe physical and mental distress, sees only bad omens, and concludes that killing his own relatives can bring no good—signaling a complete collapse of resolve.
theme: The collapse of warrior resolve under attachment and moral dread, creating the need for divine guidance

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: न काङ्क्षे विजयं कृष्ण न च राज्यं सुखानि च | किं नो राज्येन गोविन्द किं भोगैर्जीवितेन वा || 32|| येषामर्थे काङ्क्षितं नो राज्यं भोगा: सुखानि च | त इमेऽवस्थिता युद्धे प्राणांस्त्यक्त्वा धनानि च || 33||
transliteration: na kāṅkṣhe vijayaṁ kṛiṣhṇa na cha rājyaṁ sukhāni cha | kiṁ no rājyena govinda kiṁ bhogair jīvitena vā || yeṣhām arthe kāṅkṣhitaṁ no rājyaṁ bhogāḥ sukhāni cha | ta ime ’vasthitā yuddhe prāṇāṁs tyaktvā dhanāni cha ||
translation: Arjun said: O Krishna, I do not desire victory, kingdom, or the happiness that comes with them. Of what use are kingdom, pleasures, or even life itself, when those for whose sake we desire them are standing here ready for battle, having renounced their lives and wealth?
commentary_full:
Arjun’s despair deepens into renunciation of worldly rewards. He declares that victory, sovereignty, pleasure, and even life itself have lost all meaning to him if they are attained at the cost of his own relatives’ lives. The very people for whom such gains would have value are now standing before him, prepared to sacrifice everything.

From a moral standpoint, Arjun’s reasoning appears noble. Taking a life is itself grievous; killing one’s own kin feels unbearably sinful. He questions the purpose of success achieved through bloodshed and foresees only emptiness and remorse, not joy.

Yet, while Arjun’s thoughts seem detached, they are not truly spiritual. His renunciation arises from emotional attachment and compassion entangled with grief, rather than from enlightened wisdom. True spiritual detachment brings clarity, peace, and inner stability. Arjun’s state, however, is marked by confusion, distress, and loss of bodily and mental control.

Thus, these verses reveal a critical distinction: moral emotion versus spiritual understanding. Arjun stands at a crossroads—his heart rejects violence, but his mind lacks the higher vision needed to resolve this conflict. This tension sets the stage for Krishna’s forthcoming teachings on duty, detachment, and the nature of the soul.
commentary_summary: Arjun rejects victory, kingdom, pleasure, and even life if they require killing his relatives. Though morally sensitive, his renunciation is driven by grief and attachment rather than spiritual wisdom.
theme: Rejection of worldly success driven by grief and attachment rather than true spiritual detachment

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: आचार्या: पितर: पुत्रास्तथैव च पितामहा: | मातुला: श्वशुरा: पौत्रा: श्याला: सम्बन्धिनस्तथा || 34|| एतान्न हन्तुमिच्छामि घ्नतोऽपि मधुसूदन | अपि त्रैलोक्यराज्यस्य हेतो: किं नु महीकृते || 35||
transliteration: āchāryāḥ pitaraḥ putrās tathaiva cha pitāmahāḥ | mātulāḥ śhvaśhurāḥ pautrāḥ śhyālāḥ sambandhinas tathā || etān na hantum ichchhāmi ghnato ’pi madhusūdana | api trailokya-rājyasya hetoḥ kiṁ nu mahī-kṛite ||
translation: Arjun said: Teachers, fathers, sons, grandfathers, maternal uncles, grandsons, fathers-in-law, brothers-in-law, and other relatives are present here, ready to give up their lives and wealth. O Madhusudan, I do not wish to kill them, even if they try to kill me. What pleasure could we derive from ruling the three worlds—what to speak of this Earth—if we kill our own people?
commentary_full:
Arjun’s grief deepens as he mentally traces his relationships with the warriors assembled on the battlefield. He sees not soldiers, but teachers who shaped his life, elders worthy of reverence, close relatives bound by blood, marriage, and friendship. The battlefield becomes a tragic gathering of family rather than a field of righteous combat.

Addressing Krishna as *Madhusudan*, the slayer of the demon Madhu, Arjun contrasts divine justice with human attachment. He emphasizes twice the word *api* (even though), highlighting his moral stand: even if these relatives are willing to kill him, even if supreme power over the three worlds were offered, he refuses to commit such violence.

Arjun’s reasoning is deeply emotional and ethical. He questions the very value of power, dominion, and success if attained through the slaughter of one’s own kin. To him, such victory would be hollow, stained with irreversible guilt and sorrow.

However, this stance, though morally sensitive, arises from attachment rather than spiritual clarity. Arjun’s compassion, unchecked by higher wisdom, transforms into paralysis. He cannot yet see the distinction between the eternal soul and the perishable body, nor reconcile duty with righteousness.

These verses represent the peak of Arjun’s emotional resistance to war, where love, fear, and moral revulsion overpower his sense of duty, compelling him toward surrender and the need for divine guidance.
commentary_summary: Arjun refuses to kill his relatives even for dominion over the three worlds. His compassion and attachment override his warrior duty, deepening his moral and emotional paralysis.
theme: Arjun’s rejection of violence and power due to overwhelming familial attachment and moral anguish

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: निहत्य धार्तराष्ट्रान्न: का प्रीति: स्याज्जनार्दन | पापमेवाश्रयेदस्मान्हत्वैतानाततायिन: || 36 || तस्मान्नार्हा वयं हन्तुं धार्तराष्ट्रान्स्वबान्धवान् | स्वजनं हि कथं हत्वा सुखिन: स्याम माधव || 37||
transliteration: nihatya dhārtarāṣhṭrān naḥ kā prītiḥ syāj janārdana | pāpam evāśhrayed asmān hatvaitān ātatāyinaḥ || tasmān nārhā vayaṁ hantuṁ dhārtarāṣhṭrān sa-bāndhavān | sva-janaṁ hi kathaṁ hatvā sukhinaḥ syāma mādhava ||
translation: Arjun said: O Janardana, what joy will we gain by killing the sons of Dhritarashtra? Even though they are aggressors, sin will surely come upon us if we slay them. Therefore, it is not proper for us to kill our own relatives, the sons of Dhritarashtra. O Madhava, how can we ever be happy by killing our own kinsmen?
commentary_full:
Arjun continues to justify his refusal to fight, deepening his moral argument. Addressing Krishna as Janardana, the sustainer of all beings, he questions the very purpose of victory achieved through the killing of relatives. To him, such an act can bring no joy—only guilt and inner suffering.

Even while acknowledging that the Kauravas are aggressors, Arjun insists that violence against one’s own family will inevitably result in sin. His repeated use of the phrase ‘even though’ reveals his inner conflict: despite knowing the injustice committed by the Kauravas, his conscience refuses to permit their slaughter.

Addressing Krishna again as Madhava, Arjun appeals to a higher moral sensibility. He argues that happiness cannot arise from fratricide. Wealth, power, and success obtained through the blood of loved ones would be poisoned by remorse.

Arjun’s reasoning aligns with the general moral principle of non-violence emphasized in the scriptures, which regard the taking of life as a grave sin under ordinary circumstances. However, his understanding remains incomplete. Though scriptures allow violence in extreme cases of self-defense and protection of righteousness, Arjun’s judgment is clouded by attachment and emotional fear.

Thus, these verses reveal Arjun’s moral paralysis—he recognizes wrongdoing, yet cannot accept the responsibility of corrective action. His compassion, though noble, lacks the spiritual clarity needed to reconcile duty with righteousness.
commentary_summary: Arjun argues that killing his relatives will bring only sin, not happiness, even if they are aggressors. His morality is driven by fear, guilt, and attachment rather than spiritual discernment.
theme: Arjun’s moral paralysis—rejecting righteous action due to fear of sin and attachment to family

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: यद्यप्येते न पश्यन्ति लोभोपहतचेतस: | कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं मित्रद्रोहे च पातकम् || 38|| कथं न ज्ञेयमस्माभि: पापादस्मान्निवर्तितुम् | कुलक्षयकृतं दोषं प्रपश्यद्भिर्जनार्दन || 39||
transliteration: yady apy ete na paśhyanti lobhopahata-chetasaḥ | kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ mitra-drohe cha pātakam || kathaṁ na jñeyam asmābhiḥ pāpād asmān nivartitum | kula-kṣhaya-kṛitaṁ doṣhaṁ prapaśhyadbhir janārdana ||
translation: Arjun said: Even though their minds are overpowered by greed and they see no fault in destroying their own families or betraying friends, O Janardana, why should we—who clearly perceive the sin in annihilating our kindred—not turn away from this wrongdoing?
commentary_full:
Arjun sharpens his moral argument by contrasting two states of consciousness. He observes that the Kauravas are blinded by greed, which has clouded their judgment. Because of this, they fail to recognize the grave sin involved in destroying their own lineage and betraying close relations.

Arjun then turns the lens inward. Addressing Krishna as *Janardana*, the sustainer and protector of society, he asks why those who can clearly perceive the consequences of such actions should willingly participate in them. Knowledge, he argues, carries responsibility. Seeing the crime of family destruction (*kula-kṣhaya*), it would be wrong to proceed knowingly.

This reasoning reveals Arjun’s deeply ethical temperament. He believes moral awareness should lead to moral restraint. To him, abstaining from sin is superior to winning a war driven by greed.

Yet, while Arjun’s logic is ethically sensitive, it remains incomplete. His conclusion still rests on emotional aversion and attachment rather than a comprehensive understanding of duty (*dharma*) in extraordinary circumstances. He sees the sin of destruction clearly, but not yet the higher responsibility of restoring righteousness when adharma prevails.

Thus, these verses crystallize Arjun’s stance: conscious avoidance of sin through withdrawal. This position, though noble in intent, sets the stage for Krishna to later explain the deeper, action-oriented path of selfless duty guided by spiritual wisdom.
commentary_summary: Arjun argues that while the Kauravas are blinded by greed, he and the Pandavas can see the sin in destroying family. Therefore, he believes they should withdraw from the war to avoid wrongdoing.
theme: Moral awareness versus duty—Arjun’s insistence on avoiding sin by withdrawing from action

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: कुलक्षये प्रणश्यन्ति कुलधर्मा: सनातना: | धर्मे नष्टे कुलं कृत्स्नमधर्मोऽभिभवत्युत || 40||
transliteration: kula-kṣhaye praṇaśhyanti kula-dharmāḥ sanātanāḥ | dharme naṣhṭe kulaṁ kṛitsnam adharmo ’bhibhavaty uta ||
translation: Arjun said: When a dynasty is destroyed, its eternal family traditions perish, and when such traditions are lost, the entire family is overtaken by irreligion.
commentary_full:
Arjun now expands his argument beyond personal grief to societal consequences. He reflects on the destruction of a dynasty (*kula-kṣhaya*) and its far-reaching impact on moral and spiritual order. According to him, families are the carriers of *sanātana kula-dharma*—timeless traditions, values, customs, and ethical conduct passed down through generations.

The elders of a family act as custodians of wisdom and righteous living. Through guidance, example, and discipline, they preserve social harmony and moral clarity. When such elders perish prematurely in war, their accumulated knowledge and values disappear with them.

In the absence of proper guidance, the younger generations lose direction. As family traditions erode, discipline weakens and irreligion (*adharma*) begins to dominate. Arjun fears that this moral collapse will not remain confined to one family but will spread across society, leading to chaos, degradation of values, and spiritual decline.

Thus, Arjun views the killing of elders not merely as an individual tragedy, but as a civilizational loss. His concern highlights the interconnectedness of family, morality, and social order, reinforcing his belief that war will unleash irreversible harm far beyond the battlefield.
commentary_summary: Arjun warns that destroying a dynasty leads to the loss of family traditions, which in turn causes moral decay and the rise of irreligion in society.
theme: The destruction of family traditions leading to moral and social collapse

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: अधर्माभिभवात्कृष्ण प्रदुष्यन्ति कुलस्त्रिय: | स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु वार्ष्णेय जायते वर्णसङ्कर: || 41||
transliteration: adharmābhibhavāt kṛiṣhṇa praduṣhyanti kula-striyaḥ | strīṣhu duṣhṭāsu vārṣhṇeya jāyate varṇa-saṅkaraḥ ||
translation: Arjun said: With the rise of irreligion, O Krishna, the women of the family become corrupted; and when women are corrupted, O descendant of the Vrishni dynasty, unwanted progeny are born.
commentary_full:
Arjun extends his concern from family and elders to the moral stability of society at large. He argues that when *adharma* prevails, it first erodes the protective framework of family life. Without the guidance and restraint provided by elders and traditions, the moral environment deteriorates, placing the women of the family at risk of exploitation and misguidance.

In the Vedic worldview, women were revered as the moral anchors of family and society. Their well-being and virtue were seen as essential for preserving harmony, continuity, and spiritual order. Arjun fears that widespread irreligion will compromise this moral foundation, leading to social disorder.

From such disorder arises *varṇa-saṅkara*—unwanted progeny—symbolizing the breakdown of social responsibilities, lineage duties, and ritual continuity. Arjun worries that this collapse will not only disrupt future generations but also sever ancestral rites, destabilize social cohesion, and erode dharmic life.

Thus, his argument frames war as a catalyst for long-term civilizational decay. While driven by compassion and social conscience, Arjun’s reasoning still reflects fear of consequences rather than clarity about righteous duty in extraordinary times.
commentary_summary: Arjun warns that irreligion corrupts family life, endangers women, and results in social disorder through unwanted progeny, threatening long-term moral and cultural stability.
theme: Fear of societal and moral collapse due to irreligion and breakdown of family order

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: सङ्करो नरकायैव कुलघ्नानां कुलस्य च | पतन्ति पितरो ह्येषां लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रिया: || 42||
transliteration: saṅkaro narakāyaiva kula-ghnānāṁ kulasya cha | patanti pitaro hy eṣhāṁ lupta-piṇḍodaka-kriyāḥ ||
translation: Arjun said: The increase of unwanted progeny leads to a hellish existence for those who destroy the family and for the family itself. Deprived of ritual offerings, the ancestors of such families also fall from their elevated state.
commentary_full:
Arjun continues his chain of reasoning by describing the ultimate spiritual consequences of family destruction. He asserts that *varṇa-saṅkara*—unwanted or socially unanchored progeny—creates conditions of suffering not only in this life but also in the hereafter. Such disorder, according to him, condemns both the perpetrators of family destruction and their lineage to a hellish state.

A crucial concern Arjun raises is the fate of the ancestors (*pitaraḥ*). In Vedic tradition, descendants have a sacred duty to perform *piṇḍa* (food offerings) and *udaka* (water oblations) to sustain the departed ancestors in their posthumous journey. When family structures collapse and traditions are lost, these rites cease.

Deprived of these offerings, the ancestors are believed to fall from their honored positions, suffering due to neglect. Thus, Arjun perceives war not merely as physical violence, but as a rupture in the cosmic and ritual order connecting past, present, and future generations.

This verse reflects Arjun’s profound sense of responsibility toward lineage, tradition, and ancestral duty. However, his fear-driven logic again stems from attachment and ritual anxiety rather than an understanding of the eternal nature of the soul and higher dharma. Still, it vividly illustrates the depth of his moral and spiritual distress.
commentary_summary: Arjun warns that destroying families results in hellish suffering for both descendants and ancestors, as ritual duties cease and ancestral welfare is harmed.
theme: Fear of spiritual and ancestral downfall due to destruction of family traditions

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: दोषैरेतै: कुलघ्नानां वर्णसङ्करकारकै: | उत्साद्यन्ते जातिधर्मा: कुलधर्माश्च शाश्वता: || 43||
transliteration: doṣhair etaiḥ kula-ghnānāṁ varṇa-saṅkara-kārakaiḥ | utsādyante jāti-dharmāḥ kula-dharmāśh cha śhāśhvatāḥ ||
translation: Arjun said: By the misdeeds of those who destroy family traditions and thereby cause unwanted progeny, both social welfare practices and eternal family traditions are ruined.
commentary_full:
Arjun concludes his chain of reasoning by emphasizing the irreversible damage caused by the destruction of family structures. He asserts that when individuals commit grave wrongs such as annihilating their own lineage, the consequences ripple outward through society.

The emergence of *varṇa-saṅkara*—unwanted progeny—symbolizes a breakdown in moral responsibility, social roles, and ethical continuity. According to Arjun, such a breakdown does not remain limited to private family life; it erodes *jāti-dharma* (social duties and welfare activities) and *kula-dharma* (family traditions) that have been preserved for generations.

These dharmic systems are described as *śhāśhvata*—timeless—because they provide stability, identity, and moral guidance to society. When they are destroyed, communities lose their ethical compass, social cohesion weakens, and collective welfare deteriorates.

Arjun’s argument reaches its philosophical peak here: war, in his view, is not merely a violent event but a catalyst for long-term social decay. Although his concern reflects deep social conscience and moral sensitivity, it is still driven by fear of collapse and attachment to tradition, rather than an understanding of higher, contextual dharma.

This verse solidifies Arjun’s position of withdrawal and sets the stage for his eventual surrender to Krishna’s wisdom, where a deeper, action-oriented interpretation of dharma will be revealed.
commentary_summary: Arjun warns that destroying family traditions leads to unwanted progeny and ultimately ruins both social welfare systems and long-standing family values.
theme: The ruin of social and family welfare through the destruction of timeless traditions

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणां मनुष्याणां जनार्दन | नरकेऽनियतं वासो भवतीत्यनुशुश्रुम || 44||
transliteration: utsanna-kula-dharmāṇāṁ manuṣhyāṇāṁ janārdana | narake ’niyataṁ vāso bhavatīty anuśhuśhruma ||
translation: Arjun said: O Janardan, I have heard from the learned that those who destroy family traditions dwell in hell for an indefinite period of time.
commentary_full:
Arjun concludes his argument with an appeal to scriptural authority and traditional wisdom. Addressing Krishna as *Janardana*, the protector and sustainer of society, he states that he has heard from wise elders and learned scholars that the destruction of family traditions leads to prolonged suffering in hell.

In Arjun’s understanding, *kula-dharma* is not merely a social custom but a sacred responsibility linking generations. When these traditions are destroyed, individuals sever their connection to righteous living, ancestral duties, and spiritual discipline. Such actions, he believes, carry karmic consequences extending beyond this life.

The phrase *aniyataṁ vāsaḥ*—indefinite residence—reflects Arjun’s deep fear of irreversible spiritual punishment. He is not only concerned about immediate sin but also about long-term karmic repercussions that cannot be easily undone.

This verse represents the climax of Arjun’s moral and emotional reasoning in Chapter 1. His fear of hellish consequences, societal collapse, and ancestral suffering leaves him completely paralyzed. Though grounded in traditional belief, his argument is driven by anxiety and attachment rather than by clarity of higher dharma.

Having exhausted all justifications against fighting, Arjun stands at the brink of surrender, creating the necessary condition for Krishna’s forthcoming teachings on the soul, duty, and transcendental wisdom.
commentary_summary: Arjun fears that destroying family traditions leads to long-term hellish suffering. His argument, rooted in scriptural hearing, marks the peak of his moral fear and inner paralysis.
theme: Fear of karmic and hellish consequences due to the destruction of family traditions

speaker: Arjuna
text_sanskrit: अहो बत महत्पापं कर्तुं व्यवसिता वयम् | यद्राज्यसुखलोभेन हन्तुं स्वजनमुद्यता: || 45|| यदि मामप्रतीकारमशस्त्रं शस्त्रपाणय: | धार्तराष्ट्रा रणे हन्युस्तन्मे क्षेमतरं भवेत् || 46||
transliteration: aho bata mahat pāpaṁ kartuṁ vyavasitā vayam | yad rājya-sukha-lobhena hantuṁ sva-janam udyatāḥ || yadi mām apratīkāram aśhastraṁ śhastra-pāṇayaḥ | dhārtarāṣhṭrā raṇe hanyus tan me kṣhemataraṁ bhavet ||
translation: Arjun said: Alas! How grievous it is that we have resolved to commit such a great sin—driven by the greed for kingdom and pleasure, we are prepared to kill our own relatives. It would be far better for me if the sons of Dhritarashtra, weapons in hand, were to kill me unarmed and unresisting on the battlefield.
commentary_full:
Arjun reaches the lowest point of despair and self-condemnation. With the exclamation *aho* (alas), he laments the tragic irony of the situation—despite clearly foreseeing destruction, sorrow, and moral ruin, he believes that they are still being pulled toward this great sin by the lure of power and pleasure.

He harshly judges not only the Kauravas but also himself and the Pandavas, accusing them of being motivated by *rājya-sukha-lobha*—greed for royal enjoyment. This reveals how deeply guilt has overtaken his mind. Instead of recognizing the necessity of restraining injustice, Arjun internalizes blame and turns it inward.

His delusion reaches an extreme when he declares that it would be better for him to lay down his weapons and allow himself to be killed unresisting. This is no longer compassion—it is moral collapse. The warrior’s duty (*kṣhatriya-dharma*) to protect righteousness has been completely eclipsed by emotional attachment and fear of sin.

Arjun fails to recognize that allowing aggressors to triumph unchecked would cause far greater harm to society. His willingness to accept death rather than act reflects a profound confusion between true non-violence and escapism. This state of helpless surrender signals that Arjun can no longer resolve the conflict through reasoning alone.

Thus, these verses mark the culmination of Arjun’s delusion (*moha*). His intellect is overwhelmed, his will broken, and his identity as a warrior dissolved—creating the critical moment where divine instruction becomes indispensable.
commentary_summary: Arjun condemns himself for being driven by greed and prefers death over killing his relatives. His despair peaks, showing total collapse of courage and clarity, making divine guidance necessary.
theme: Arjun’s complete moral and emotional collapse, preferring death over duty, necessitating divine guidance

speaker: Sanjaya
text_sanskrit: सञ्जय उवाच | एवमुक्त्वार्जुन: सङ्ख्ये रथोपस्थ उपाविशत् | विसृज्य सशरं चापं शोकसंविग्नमानस: || 47||
transliteration: sañjaya uvācha | evam uktvārjunaḥ saṅkhye rathopastha upāviśhat | visṛijya sa-śharaṁ chāpaṁ śhoka-saṁvigna-mānasaḥ ||
translation: Sanjay said: Having spoken thus on the battlefield, Arjun cast aside his bow and arrows and sat down on the seat of his chariot, his mind overwhelmed with grief.
commentary_full:
With this verse, the first chapter of the Bhagavad Gita comes to a close, marking the complete collapse of Arjun’s resolve. After articulating all his fears, moral arguments, and emotional justifications, Arjun finally abandons action altogether. Casting aside his famed bow and arrows, he sinks down upon his chariot seat, physically mirroring his inner defeat.

Arjun’s grief has now fully overtaken him. His mind is described as *śhoka-saṁvigna*—deeply agitated and distressed by sorrow. This is not merely sadness but a profound paralysis born of attachment, fear of sin, and confusion about duty. His refusal to fight is no longer intellectual; it has become total emotional surrender.

This moment is striking because Arjun is no ordinary warrior. He had earlier chosen the unarmed Shree Krishna over vast armies, demonstrating supreme devotion and faith. He was the son of Indra, recipient of celestial weapons, and in a previous incarnation, part of the divine twin Nar–Nārāyaṇa. Yet, even such an exalted soul succumbs to delusion when overwhelmed by material attachment.

This collapse is intentional and meaningful. For the welfare of future generations, Shree Krishna allows Arjun to descend fully into confusion so that divine wisdom may arise from this crisis. Arjun’s silence and inaction now create the perfect opening for Krishna’s teachings.

Thus ends *Arjuna Viṣhāda Yoga*—the Yoga of Arjun’s Despair. From this darkness of sorrow and helplessness will emerge the luminous philosophy of the Bhagavad Gita, guiding humanity on duty, devotion, knowledge, and liberation.
commentary_summary: Arjun drops his weapons and sits down in despair, completely overwhelmed by grief. His moral and emotional collapse concludes Chapter 1 and prepares the ground for Krishna’s divine teachings.
theme: The culmination of Arjun’s despair and surrender, setting the stage for the Bhagavad Gita’s teachings
